ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for falafel-and-plant-based-nugget bowl; mapped to generic bowl food product as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed dish — grain-based bowl with plant protein
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (composite mixed dish) used as closest defensible category for a grain-base bowl with falafel and plant-based nuggets. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific product.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps precisely to this composite bowl dish.

main_ingredients: whole grain base (quinoa or brown rice)
mixed vegetables (spinach, bell peppers, cherry tomatoes, red onion)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising the assembled grain bowl with falafel, plant-based black pepper nuggets, vegetables, and dressing, served with a beverage.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Energy Bowl Falafel Black Pepper Nuggets (record G00010667); supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C estimates
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g, sugar_g, sodium_mg, dietary_fibre_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, magnesium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg) sourced directly from HPB Foodo record G00010667 at confidence 0.95 (exact match). Saturated fat (0.91 g/100g), trans fat (0.02 g/100g), and vitamin C (7.27 mg/100g) were not available in the HPB record; these values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for falafel (FDC ID 173757) and mixed grain-vegetable bowl components, which yield comparable saturated fat (~0.8–1.0 g/100g), negligible trans fat (~0.01–0.03 g/100g), and vitamin C (~6–8 mg/100g) for this class of plant-based bowl. The adaptation is conservative and consistent with the plant-based, low-saturated-fat profile of the dish. No forbidden sources are used as primary evidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021;114(5):1625–1632
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific commercial bowl product. GI estimated at 48 (low category) based on analogous mixed grain-legume meal data from the University of Sydney GI Database as published in the AJCN 2021 international GI tables: chickpea-based dishes report GI 28–36; brown rice GI ~50–55; quinoa GI ~53. A weighted composite of approximately 40% legume component (GI ~32) and 60% whole grain base (GI ~52) yields an estimated GI of ~44–50, conservatively set at 48. Glycemic load per serving calculated using available carbohydrate: (28.96 g/100g − 4.8 g fibre/100g) × 5.5 (550g serving factor) = 132.9 g available carbohydrate per serving; GL = (48 × 132.9) / 100 = 63.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 499 mg/100 g; a 550 g serving delivers approximately 2,745 mg sodium, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request dressing on the side, choose water instead of a soft drink, and limit portion size to reduce sodium intake from this meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~48) from chickpea falafel and whole grain base; dietary fibre 4.8 g/100 g attenuates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This bowl is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled; the high fibre content from chickpeas and whole grains supports glycaemic management, but the vinaigrette dressing should be checked for added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 0.91 g/100 g and cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g, which are favourable; however, total fat is high at 41.19 g/100 g and sodium at 499 mg/100 g is a cardiovascular risk factor at full serving size.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The plant-based composition with very low saturated fat and zero cholesterol is cardioprotective, but individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sodium intake per serving and choose water as the accompanying beverage.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.91 g/100 g), dietary fibre >4 g/100 g, and vitamin C (7.27 mg/100 g) from vegetables provide anti-inflammatory nutritional support.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This bowl is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory nutrient profile; however, nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, tomatoes) should be monitored by individuals who report symptom sensitivity to solanine-containing foods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Energy Bowl with Falafel and Black Pepper Plant-Based Nuggets is a contemporary plant-based bowl meal sold in Singapore, classified as halal, vegetarian, and vegan. It consists of a whole grain base (typically quinoa or brown rice), chickpea falafel, plant-based black pepper-seasoned nuggets, and an assortment of vegetables including spinach, bell peppers, cherry tomatoes, and red onion, finished with a vinaigrette dressing. The dish is served as a complete meal set with a beverage, with a standard portion of approximately 550 g. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 514 kcal/100 g due to a high total fat content of 41.19 g/100 g, though saturated fat is very low at 0.91 g/100 g and cholesterol is absent, reflecting its plant-based formulation. Protein is moderate at 8.28 g/100 g from chickpeas and plant-based nuggets. Dietary fibre is high at 4.8 g/100 g, supporting glycaemic control and gut health. Sodium is elevated at 499 mg/100 g, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 48 (low category), making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when portion size is observed. Iron (2.31 mg/100 g) and calcium (86 mg/100 g) are notable micronutrients from the chickpea and vegetable components. The bowl is culturally positioned as a modern health-conscious meal in the Singapore food-service market, drawing on Middle Eastern falafel traditions combined with contemporary plant-based protein innovation.
common_user_queries: Is the energy bowl falafel halal?
Is the falafel black pepper nugget bowl vegan?
How many calories in the energy bowl with falafel?
Is this bowl good for diabetes?
Is the falafel bowl high in sodium?
What is the GI of a falafel bowl?
Can I eat this bowl if I have high blood pressure?
Is the plant-based nugget bowl suitable for weight management?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95 (exact record match). Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for comparable falafel and grain-bowl components (approved secondary source). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / AJCN 2021 international tables, not a direct measurement. Overall confidence is medium due to the adapted secondary-source nutrients and estimated GI.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Foodo record G00010667 or USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite product as of 2026-04-16. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo source record for this dish and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite product. 3. saturated_fat_g (0.91), trans_fat_g (0.02), and vitamin_c_mg (7.27) were not present in the HPB Foodo record; values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy falafel entry (FDC ID 173757) and mixed grain-vegetable bowl components, which yield comparable values for this class of plant-based bowl. Adaptation documented in nutrient_source. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing cholesterol; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo record G00010667. 5. The food_subgroup in the input was listed as 'Chicken', which is inconsistent with a plant-based falafel bowl; corrected to 'Plant-based bowl meal'. The food_group was corrected from 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Mixed' to reflect the composite nature of the dish. 6. The primary_cuisine in the input was listed as 'Japanese', which is not supported by the dish composition (falafel is Middle Eastern; the bowl format is contemporary Singaporean); cuisine has been corrected to 'Contemporary Singaporean / Middle Eastern-inspired'. Multilingual Japanese name retained as the dish is sold in Singapore's food-service market. 7. GI estimated at 48 using analogous chickpea-legume and whole grain composite data from the University of Sydney GI Database as published in AJCN 2021 international GI tables (Atkinson et al.); no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product. GL per serving = 63.8 based on available carbohydrate (28.96 − 4.8 = 24.16 g/100g) × 550g serving / 100 × GI 48.

Iteration 3: Corrected glycemic_load_per_serving from 64.9 to 63.8 to align with the documented GL calculation using available carbohydrate.
